narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, an LSAT score of 34, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school deciles from Year 1 (3rd decile) and Year 3 (1st decile). The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 42% chance that you would fail it on your first try—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key aspects of your background that stood out the most. The strongest factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking in the bottom 1st decile, well below the typical 5th or 6th decile for most students, signaling to the model a significant dip in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Counterbalancing that, your undergraduate GPA of 3.50—stronger than the average of 3.21—strongly pulled the prediction toward success, showing the model your solid academic foundation from college. Your LSAT score of 34, while below the dataset average of 36.7, also nudged things positively by demonstrating decent standardized reasoning skills compared to lower performers. Overall, your low Year 3 decile outweighed these strengths, tipping the scales against you in a way that's tougher for applicants with similar late-stage grade drops, regardless of background. Many students with steadier decile progressions, even with average LSATs, get favorable nods because the model sees their trajectories as more bar-ready. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average or strengthening recent academics through post-bac courses or a master's program to improve your decile standing. Gathering letters from professors who can highlight your Year 3 turnaround potential could also help reframe your story. Reapplying next cycle with these enhancements, perhaps targeting schools with holistic reviews that value your Hispanic perspective in law, gives you a real shot at flipping this outcome. You're clearly capable—your UGPA proves it—so focus on building that momentum now.
